title: Movement Analysis II
text:
library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(tmap)
library(zoo)
library(vip)
library(sfnetworks)

title: Movement Analysis II
section: No Free Lunch Theorem – Choosing a Model
text:
No Free Lunch Theorem – Choosing a Model
In machine learning, an important theorem states that there is no universally best algorithm. Different algorithms perform differently depending on the feature space, and no single model outperforms all others in every situation. It is therefore important to evaluate multiple algorithms, since it is not clear in advance which one will perform best.
Ideally, this evaluation would be done exhaustively — varying all hyperparameters for every algorithm. In practice, however, there is always a tension between computational resources and performance. Running a full hyperparameter grid search combined with cross-validation is simply too expensive in terms of execution time. A greedy search is deployed here instead: all models are run with their default parameters and evaluated with a 5-fold cross-validation. This approach will identify a good algorithm, but not necessarily the optimal one. KNN, for instance, could outperform XGBoost for this problem, but may not be discovered as the better choice if its default hyperparameters are less suited than those of XGBoost for this particular task.

data_split <- full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~if_else(is.infinite(.), NA_real_, .))) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_initial_split(., prop = 0.75, group = track_id, strata = mode)

production_set <- data_split %>% training()
testing_set    <- data_split %>% testing()


Preparing the Recipe
In tidymodels, a recipe defines the preprocessing pipeline. Its key advantage is that it integrates cleanly into cross-validation frameworks: the complete preprocessing is applied separately to each training and test fold. This matters because some preprocessing steps — such as feature elimination based on correlation — would otherwise leak information from the training set into the test fold. The architecture of tidymodels enforces this best practice by design.
For the train-test split, a grouped split is used to ensure that points from the same movement track do not end up in different datasets. Data points from the same track share inherent similarity due to their common origin, which would otherwise inflate performance metrics. A stratified split is also applied to preserve the relative proportions of the different transportation modes across both datasets.
The recipe performs the following steps:

Dropping predictors that suffer from multicollinearity with other predictors.
Scaling the remaining predictors to the [0, 1] range, as some models perform poorly in unscaled feature spaces.


rec <- production_set %>%
  select(!c(user_id, track_id, datetime, alt, data)) %>%
  recipe(mode ~ ., .) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.75) %>%
  step_range(all_numeric_predictors(), min = 0, max = 1)



Defining Models
tidymodels provides a uniform interface to a wide range of ML packages implemented in R. The set_engine function allows selecting the underlying package for each algorithm.
A workflow set is then defined, combining each classifier with the preprocessing recipe into individual workflows. Each workflow is evaluated using a 5-fold cross-validation to obtain robust performance metrics, and the best-performing model is selected.

#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Extreme gradient boosted trees
xgb_model <- boost_tree(trees = 500) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("xgboost", device = "cuda", tree_method = "hist")

# Multinomial logistic regression
logistic_model <- multinom_reg(penalty = 0.01) %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("glmnet")

# Decision tree
dt_model <- decision_tree() %>%
  set_mode("classification") %>%
  set_engine("rpart")

# Combine into a workflow set
wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(rec = rec),
  models  = list(xgb = xgb_model, logistic = logistic_model, dt = dt_model)
)

results <- wf_set %>%
  workflow_map(
    fn = "fit_resamples",
    resamples = group_vfold_cv(production_set, group = track_id, v = 5, strata = mode),
    metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc)
  )

rank_results(results, rank_metric = "accuracy")

  wflow_id     .config      .metric  mean std_err     n preprocessor model  rank
  <chr>        <chr>        <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <int> <chr>        <chr> <int>
1 rec_xgb      pre0_mod0_p… accura… 0.586 0.0143      5 recipe       boos…     1
2 rec_xgb      pre0_mod0_p… roc_auc 0.854 0.00493     5 recipe       boos…     1
3 rec_dt       pre0_mod0_p… accura… 0.484 0.0154      5 recipe       deci…     2
4 rec_dt       pre0_mod0_p… roc_auc 0.758 0.00246     5 recipe       deci…     2
5 rec_logistic pre0_mod0_p… accura… 0.441 0.0173      5 recipe       mult…     3
6 rec_logistic pre0_mod0_p… roc_auc 0.741 0.0204      5 recipe       mult…     3


Gradient boosting outperformed the other algorithms by a clear margin, so the analysis continues with gradient boosted trees.


Continuing with XGBoost
Next, hyperparameter tuning is performed for XGBoost as well as for the recipe step. The multicollinearity threshold is somewhat arbitrary and is generally best determined empirically. Tuning is carried out using a nested cross-validation with a simple grid search. To speed up training, xgboost is run with GPU support, which requires the appropriate xgboost binary, the CUDA toolkit, matching drivers, and a CUDA-capable GPU.

#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Recipe with tunable correlation threshold
rec <- production_set %>%
  select(!c(user_id, track_id, datetime, alt, data)) %>%
  recipe(mode ~ ., .) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = tune()) %>%
  step_range(all_numeric_predictors(), min = 0, max = 1)

# XGBoost with tunable hyperparameters
xgb_model <- boost_tree(
  trees      = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune(),
  min_n      = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("xgboost", device = "cuda", tree_method = "hist", nthread = 8) %>%
  set_mode("classification")

wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(rec) %>%
  add_model(xgb_model)

# 5-fold cross-validation with a grid search over 10 candidates
results <- wf %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = group_vfold_cv(production_set, group = track_id, v = 5, strata = mode),
    grid = 10
  )

Training the Final Model
With the best-performing hyperparameters identified, the model can now be trained and evaluated on the test set.

best_params <- select_best(results, metric = "roc_auc")
final_wf    <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_params)

final_fit <- final_wf %>%
  last_fit(data_split)

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  .metric     .estimator .estimate .config

final_fit %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = mode, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix – XGBoost Classifier",
    x     = "Predicted Mode",
    y     = "True Mode"
  )








Walking and bus appear to be the easiest modes to classify. Overall, there is still considerable confusion between some classes, leaving room for further improvement.



Refining the Feature Space
Environmental information can be used to enrich the feature space.

gpkg <- "data/osm.gpkg"
st_layers(gpkg)

Driver: GPKG 
Available layers:
  layer_name geometry_type features fields              crs_name
1    highway   Line String    43376      8 WGS 84 / UTM zone 50N
2    railway   Line String     4095      7 WGS 84 / UTM zone 50N

highway_data <- st_read("data/osm.gpkg", layer = "highway")

Reading layer `highway' from data source

Reading layer `railway' from data source 
  `/home/etienne/movement_analysis_II/data/osm.gpkg' using driver `GPKG'

tm_shape(highway_data) +
  tm_lines(col = "highway")









tm_shape(railway_data) +
  tm_lines(col = "railway")








The features proposed in the task description are adapted here, with several additional ones designed on top.

# Distance to nearest railway line
nearest_railway <- st_nearest_feature(full_dataset_feautre_eng, railway_data)

railway_dist <- st_distance(
  full_dataset_feautre_eng,
  railway_data[nearest_railway, ],
  by_element = TRUE
)

full_dataset_feautre_eng$distance_to_railway <- railway_dist

rm(railway_dist)
gc()

           used (Mb) gc trigger   (Mb)  max used   (Mb)
Ncells  8836831  472   22744541 1214.7  22744541 1214.7
Vcells 66451395  507  151568942 1156.4 151568942 1156.4



Distances to Road Types
Pedestrians and cyclists tend to travel closer to residential streets, while cars and buses are more commonly found near motorways. Encoding the distance to each road subcategory for every data point therefore provides a potentially rich set of spatially informative features. These are computed iteratively with garbage collection after each step to avoid memory overflows.

highway_split <- split(highway_data, highway_data$highway)

for (cat_name in names(highway_split)) {
  sub <- highway_split[[cat_name]]

  nearest  <- st_nearest_feature(full_dataset_feautre_eng, sub)
  dist_col <- st_distance(full_dataset_feautre_eng, sub[nearest, ], by_element = TRUE)

  full_dataset_feautre_eng[[paste0("dist_highway_", cat_name)]] <- as.numeric(dist_col)

  rm(sub, nearest, dist_col)
  gc()
}

rm(highway_split)
gc()

           used  (Mb) gc trigger   (Mb)  max used   (Mb)
Ncells  8837467 472.0   22744542 1214.7  22744542 1214.7
Vcells 73485965 560.7  151568942 1156.4 151568942 1156.4




Betweenness Centrality
Points associated with high betweenness centrality are near network nodes that act as bridges in the road network. Nodes that many shortest routes between other locations must pass through. A node on a major arterial road connecting two parts of the city will have high betweenness, while a node on a quiet dead-end street will have near zero, since no route between other places needs to pass through it. And these bussy roads are likely to be roads and not padestrian walks.

highway_network <- as_sfnetwork(highway_data, directed = FALSE) %>%
  activate("edges") %>%
  mutate(
    weight = pmax(as.numeric(edge_length()), 0.001)
  )

highway_network <- highway_network %>%
  activate("nodes") %>%
  mutate(
    centrality_between = tidygraph::centrality_betweenness(weights = weight, normalized = TRUE)
  )


highway_nodes <- highway_network %>%
  activate("nodes") %>%
  st_as_sf()

nearest_nodes <- st_nearest_feature(full_dataset_feautre_eng, highway_nodes)

full_dataset_feautre_eng <- full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  mutate(
    centrality_between = highway_nodes$centrality_between[nearest_nodes]
  )


tmap_mode("view")

ℹ tmap modes "plot" - "view"
ℹ toggle with `tmap::ttm()`

full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  slice_sample(n = 10000) %>%
  tm_shape() + 
  tm_dots(
    fill = "centrality_between",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.yl_or_rd"),
    title = "Closeness Centrality"
  ) +
  tm_title("Betweennes centrality Snapped to Movement Points")

[tm_dots()] Argument `title` unknown.








full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  filter(!is.na(mode)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = dist_highway_tertiary, y = dist_highway_residential, color = mode)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Distance to Tertiary vs. Residential Roads by Travel Mode",
    x     = "Distance to Nearest Tertiary Road (m)",
    y     = "Distance to Nearest Residential Road (m)"
  )








No clear separation is visible in this two-dimensional projection, though patterns may emerge in higher-dimensional space.


Encoding the Temporal Domain
While this exercise is primarily about encoding spatial information, the course is called Spatio-Temporal Data Science — and since this is the final task of the final exercise, it is now or never. The timestamp is therefore used to determine whether a data point was recorded during rush hour, as public transport modes such as bus, subway, and train are more likely to be used at those times.

full_dataset_feautre_eng$hour <- lubridate::hour(full_dataset_feautre_eng$datetime)

full_dataset_feautre_eng$is_rush_hour <- full_dataset_feautre_eng$hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 17, 18, 19)

full_dataset_feautre_eng <- full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  mutate(is_rush_hour = as.numeric(is_rush_hour))


full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  filter(!is.na(mode)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = mode, y = hour)) +
  geom_violin() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Travel Hour by Mode",
    x     = "Travel Mode",
    y     = "Hour of Day"
  )








Subway and train appear to show some concentration around rush hours, while walking is distributed much more evenly throughout the day.


Rerunning Hyperparameter Tuning
With the expanded feature set, the hyperparameter tuning is repeated to find the best configuration for the updated data.

data_split <- full_dataset_feautre_eng %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
  mutate(across(where(~inherits(., "units")), as.numeric)) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~if_else(is.infinite(.), NA_real_, .))) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_initial_split(., group = track_id, prop = 0.75, strata = mode)

production_set <- data_split %>% training()
testing_set    <- data_split %>% testing()

rec <- production_set %>%
  select(!c(user_id, track_id, datetime, alt, data)) %>%
  recipe(mode ~ ., .) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = tune()) %>%
  step_range(all_numeric_predictors(), min = 0, max = 1)

xgb_model <- boost_tree(
  trees      = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune(),
  tree_depth = tune(),
  min_n      = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("xgboost", device = "cuda", tree_method = "hist", nthread = 8) %>%
  set_mode("classification")

wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(rec) %>%
  add_model(xgb_model)

results <- wf %>%
  tune_grid(
    resamples = group_vfold_cv(production_set, group = track_id, v = 5, strata = mode),
    grid = 5
  )

show_best(results, metric = "accuracy")

# A tibble: 5 × 11
  trees min_n tree_depth learn_rate threshold .metric  .estimator  mean     n
  <int> <int>      <int>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>
1  2000     2          8    0.0750       0.75 accuracy multiclass 0.683     5
2  1500    40         15    0.00422      0.5  accuracy multiclass 0.677     5
3   500    30          1    0.0178       1    accuracy multiclass 0.564     5
4     1    21         11    0.316        0.25 accuracy multiclass 0.525     5
5  1000    11          4    0.001        0    accuracy multiclass 0.238     5
# ℹ 2 more variables: std_err <dbl>, .config <chr>

show_best(results, metric = "roc_auc")

# A tibble: 5 × 11
  trees min_n tree_depth learn_rate threshold .metric .estimator  mean     n
  <int> <int>      <int>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>
1  2000     2          8    0.0750       0.75 roc_auc hand_till  0.923     5
2  1500    40         15    0.00422      0.5  roc_auc hand_till  0.909     5
3   500    30          1    0.0178       1    roc_auc hand_till  0.860     5
4     1    21         11    0.316        0.25 roc_auc hand_till  0.796     5
5  1000    11          4    0.001        0    roc_auc hand_till  0.553     5
# ℹ 2 more variables: std_err <dbl>, .config <chr>


Performance is noticeably better with the extended feature set. Including the distance to every road subcategory essentially encodes the geometry of the city into the model, allowing it to learn spatial structure directly. Whether this is appropriate depends on the use case. In contrast to a project where generalization to a different geographic area is required — such as detecting cold water patches in a different river reach — the model here is not expected to transfer to a different city. The road network can therefore be treated as a stable environmental feature, and including it is justified.

best_params <- select_best(results, metric = "roc_auc")
final_wf    <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_params)

final_fit <- final_wf %>%
  last_fit(data_split)

→ A | warning: Passed invalid argument 'info' - entries on it should be passed as direct arguments. This warning will become an error in a future version.


→ B | warning: Passed invalid function arguments: device, tree_method, nthread, num_class. These should be passed as a list to argument 'params'. Conversion from argument to 'params' entry will be done automatically, but this behavior will become an error in a future version.


→ C | warning: Parameter 'watchlist' has been renamed to 'evals'. This warning will become an error in a future version.


→ D | warning: Argument 'objective' is only for custom objectives. For built-in objectives, pass the objective under 'params'. This warning will become an error in a future version.


There were issues with some computations   A: x1   B: x1   C: x1   D: x1


→ E | warning: ✖ No observations were detected in `truth` for levels: subway and train.
               ℹ Computation will proceed by ignoring those levels.


There were issues with some computations   A: x1   B: x1   C: x1   D: x1
There were issues with some computations   A: x2   B: x2   C: x2   D: x2   E: x1
There were issues with some computations   A: x2   B: x2   C: x2   D: x2   E: x1

collect_metrics(final_fit)

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  .metric     .estimator .estimate .config        
  <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>          
1 accuracy    multiclass     0.700 pre0_mod0_post0
2 roc_auc     hand_till      0.893 pre0_mod0_post0
3 brier_class multiclass     0.249 pre0_mod0_post0



final_fit %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = mode, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(
    title = "Confusion Matrix – XGBoost Classifier",
    x     = "Predicted Mode",
    y     = "True Mode"
  )











Feature Importance
Out of curiosity, the most influential features for the model are examined below.

final_fit %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 10 Most Important Features – XGBoost",
    x     = "Importance (Gain)",
    y     = "Feature"
  )








Speed is the most informative feature, which is unsurprising given the substantial differences in travel speed across modes. Spatial and temporal features follow, which is also expected: knowing the nearest road type encodes a great deal about the local urban structure. Also it is interesting, that it seems to be of great importance at what time the datapoint was recorded yet it is of lesser interest for the model wheter the point was recorded during rush hour.

title: Movement Analysis II
text:
Usage of AI
Claude Sonnet 4.6 was used as an aid in this project. It served as a coding assistant for debugging and querying function behaviour, helped with installing the CUDA toolkit and drivers for Windows Subsystem for Linux, and was used for spelling correction and formatting.
